questions.question: Which form of the malaria parasite initiates the hepatic exoerythrocytic cycle?
questions.explanation: The sporozoite is the infective form inoculated by the Anopheles mosquito, which migrates to the liver and invades the hepatocytes to initiate the exoerythrocytic cycle.

questions.question: What is the main product of the hepatic exoerythrocytic phase in malaria?
questions.explanation: The sporozoites transform into hepatic schizonts that release merozoites into the bloodstream, initiating the erythrocytic phase.

questions.question: What species of Plasmodium can remain dormant in the liver as a hypnozoite?
questions.explanation: Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium ovale produce hypnozoites, latent liver forms that cause relapses months or years after initial infection.

questions.question: How long does the hepatic exoerythrocytic cycle typically last in Plasmodium falciparum malaria?
questions.explanation: In Plasmodium falciparum the liver phase lasts approximately 5 to 7 days, after which the merozoites are released into the bloodstream.

questions.question: Which drug is effective against Plasmodium vivax hepatic hypnozoites?
questions.explanation: Primaquine is the drug of choice to eliminate hepatic hypnozoites, preventing relapses; Chloroquine and artemisinin do not act in this phase.

questions.question: What is the immediate consequence of rupture of the hepatic schizont in malaria?
questions.explanation: Rupture of the hepatic schizont releases merozoites into the bloodstream, which then invade red blood cells and initiate the symptomatic erythrocytic phase.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of cerebral malaria due to Plasmodium falciparum?
questions.explanation: Sequestration of parasitized erythrocytes in the cerebral microvasculature, mediated by adhesins such as PfEMP1, is the central mechanism causing obstruction and endothelial damage in cerebral malaria.

questions.question: Which clinical finding is characteristic of Plasmodium falciparum cerebral malaria?
questions.explanation: Seizures and severe headache are common neurological manifestations in cerebral malaria, along with altered level of consciousness, although coma is the defining sign.

questions.question: What parasite is the main etiological agent of cerebral malaria?
questions.explanation: Plasmodium falciparum is the species that most frequently causes cerebral malaria, due to its capacity for erythrocyte sequestration and cytoadhesion in the cerebral microvasculature.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for Plasmodium falciparum cerebral malaria?
questions.explanation: Intravenous artemisinin (artesunate) is the treatment of choice for severe cerebral malaria due to Plasmodium falciparum, due to its rapid action and lower mortality compared to quinine.

questions.question: What late neurological complication can occur after cerebral malaria due to Plasmodium falciparum?
questions.explanation: Residual cognitive deficits, including memory, attention, and language problems, are a recognized neurological sequelae in children and adults who survive severe cerebral malaria.

questions.question: What characteristic of Plasmodium falciparum favors the development of cerebral malaria?
questions.explanation: The expression of the PfEMP1 protein on the surface of the parasitized erythrocyte allows adhesion to the cerebral endothelium, facilitating vascular sequestration and the pathogenesis of cerebral malaria.

questions.question: What is the typical interval of febrile attacks in P. vivax malaria?
questions.explanation: Tertian fever due to P. vivax is characterized by febrile attacks that occur every 48 hours, coinciding with the release of merozoites into the bloodstream.

questions.question: Which of the following parasites causes benign tertian fever?
questions.explanation: Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium ovale are the causative agents of benign tertian fever, with 48-hour cycles, unlike P. falciparum, which causes malignant tertian fever.

questions.question: What parasitic form is responsible for relapses in P. vivax malaria?
questions.explanation: Hypnozoites are latent intrahepatic forms of P. vivax and P. ovale that can reactivate weeks or months later, causing clinical relapses of the disease.

questions.question: What is the treatment of choice to eliminate P. vivax hypnozoites?
questions.explanation: Primaquine is the drug of choice for the eradication of hepatic hypnozoites of P. vivax and P. ovale, thus preventing relapses of tertian malaria.

questions.question: What species of Plasmodium has a tertian febrile cycle but with quartan fever?
questions.explanation: Plasmodium malariae causes quartan fever (every 72 hours), unlike P. vivax and P. ovale which cause tertian fever (every 48 hours), so the statement is incorrect for the context.

questions.question: Which of the following findings is characteristic of peripheral blood smear in P. vivax malaria?
questions.explanation: In P. vivax malaria, the parasitized erythrocytes are usually enlarged (macrocytes), unlike P. falciparum, which has multiple ring shapes and banana gametocytes.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of anemia in Plasmodium falciparum malaria?
questions.explanation: Direct rupture of erythrocytes during schizogony produces intravascular hemolysis, releasing hemoglobin into the plasma and contributing to acute anemia.

questions.question: Which laboratory finding is characteristic of malaria hemolytic anemia?
questions.explanation: Hemolysis of erythrocytes increases indirect bilirubin due to heme degradation, along with an increase in LDH and reticulocytes, and a decrease in haptoglobin.

questions.question: What acute complication can trigger massive hemolysis in severe malaria?
questions.explanation: Free hemoglobin obstructs the renal tubules and causes acute tubular necrosis, leading to acute renal failure in severe falciparum malaria.

questions.question: What antimalarial treatment is indicated in severe hemolytic anemia due to P. falciparum?
questions.explanation: Artemether-lumefantrine is a first-line treatment for malaria not complicated by P. falciparum, controlling parasitaemia and stopping hemolysis.

questions.question: Which Plasmodium falciparum virulence factor promotes erythrocyte rupture?
questions.explanation: The PfEMP1 protein mediates the adhesion of infected erythrocytes to the endothelium, preventing splenic clearance and facilitating rupture during schizogony.

questions.question: What is the direct consequence of the breakdown of erythrocytes in the microcirculation?
questions.explanation: Hemolysis reduces oxygen transport capacity, generating hypoxia in distal tissues, especially in organs such as the kidney and brain.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of quinine hypoglycemia in malaria?
questions.explanation: Quinine stimulates insulin secretion by pancreatic beta cells, which can cause severe hypoglycemia, especially in patients with severe malaria.

questions.question: Which antimalarial drug is associated with a higher risk of hypoglycemia?
questions.explanation: Quinine is the antimalarial that most frequently causes hypoglycemia due to its stimulating effect on insulin secretion, unlike other drugs such as chloroquine or mefloquine.

questions.question: In severe malaria, quinine-induced hypoglycemia typically manifests as:
questions.explanation: Severe hypoglycemia due to quinine can cause neuroglycopenic symptoms such as seizures and coma, which can be confused with cerebral malaria itself, making diagnosis difficult.

questions.question: Which group of malaria patients is at greatest risk of hypoglycemia from quinine?
questions.explanation: Pregnant women with malaria are at increased risk of quinine hypoglycemia due to physiological changes in glucose metabolism and increased insulin sensitivity.

questions.question: What is the initial management of hypoglycemia due to quinine in malaria?
questions.explanation: Intravenous administration of 10% dextrose is the treatment of choice to correct acute hypoglycemia, maintaining quinine if necessary for parasite control.

questions.question: Which laboratory finding confirms quinine-induced hypoglycemia?
questions.explanation: Hypoglycemia is biochemically defined as serum glucose less than 70 mg/dL, and in the context of quinine treatment it should be suspected even if the patient is asymptomatic.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of artemisinin?
questions.explanation: Artemisinin acts by generating oxygen free radicals by reacting with the iron of the heme group, damaging proteins and membranes of the parasite.

questions.question: What role does lumefantrine play in combination therapy?
options: Acts as a late schizonticide agent
Inhibits the synthesis of nucleic acids
questions.explanation: Lumefantrine is a long-acting blood schizonticide that eliminates residual parasites after the rapid action of artemisinin.

questions.question: What is the main indication for artemisinin-lumefantrine?
options: Malaria not complicated by P. falciparum
questions.explanation: Artemisinin-lumefantrine is the first-line treatment for uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in endemic regions.

questions.question: What is a common side effect of artemisinin-lumefantrine?
questions.explanation: Lumefantrine can prolong the QT interval, so it should be avoided in patients with cardiac conduction disorders or use of drugs that potentiate it.

questions.question: What parasite causes malaria that is treated with artemisinin-lumefantrine?
questions.explanation: Artemisinin-lumefantrine is indicated mainly against Plasmodium falciparum, the most lethal species and resistant to other antimalarials.

questions.question: What dietary recommendation is associated with the administration of artemisinin-lumefantrine?
options: Avoid foods rich in vitamin C
Take with milk or fatty foods
questions.explanation: Fatty foods increase the absorption of lumefantrine, improving its bioavailability and therapeutic efficacy in the treatment of malaria.

questions.question: What is the method of choice for diagnosing malaria in endemic areas?
questions.explanation: The thick smear is the gold standard technique in the field due to its high sensitivity to detect parasites even with low parasitemia, allowing a rapid and economical diagnosis.

questions.question: What stain is used to identify Plasmodium in blood smear?
questions.explanation: The Giemsa stain is the most used for the microscopic diagnosis of malaria, since it stains the nucleus and cytoplasm of the parasite, facilitating the differentiation of Plasmodium species.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of the thick smear over the thin smear in malaria?
questions.explanation: The thick smear concentrates a greater volume of blood, which increases the sensitivity to detect low parasitemias, although it does not allow the species to be identified with as much precision as the thin smear.

questions.question: What finding in the thin smear allows you to differentiate Plasmodium falciparum from other species?
questions.explanation: Crescent-shaped (banana) gametocytes are characteristic of Plasmodium falciparum, while other species have rounded gametocytes, facilitating microscopic identification.

questions.question: What is the minimum detectable parasitemia per thick smear under optimal conditions?
questions.explanation: The thick smear can detect up to 5 parasites per microliter of blood in expert hands, which makes it superior to the thin smear (approximately 50 parasites/μL) for low parasitemias.

questions.question: What artifact can be confused with Plasmodium in the thick smear?
questions.explanation: Overlapping platelets can simulate small Plasmodium trophozoites, especially in poorly stained samples or with low parasitemia, being a common cause of false positives in microscopy.

questions.question: What is the main adverse effect of mefloquine in antimalarial chemoprophylaxis?
questions.explanation: Mefloquine is associated with neuropsychiatric alterations such as anxiety, insomnia and seizures, being the most relevant adverse effect that limits its prophylactic use.

questions.question: What group of patients has an absolute contraindication to receiving mefloquine as prophylaxis?
questions.explanation: Mefloquine is contraindicated in patients with epilepsy or a history of seizures due to its neurotoxic potential and risk of triggering epileptic seizures.

questions.question: What is the standard weekly dose of mefloquine for adults in antimalarial chemoprophylaxis?
questions.explanation: The standard prophylactic dose of mefloquine for adults is 250 mg orally once weekly, starting at least one week before exposure.

questions.question: What mechanism of action do mefloquine and doxycycline share against Plasmodium?
questions.explanation: Doxycycline acts by inhibiting the parasite's ribosomal protein synthesis, while mefloquine blocks heme polymerization; They do not share an exact mechanism.

questions.question: What is the main limitation of the use of doxycycline in antimalarial chemoprophylaxis?
questions.explanation: Doxycycline causes significant skin photosensitivity, forcing patients to avoid direct sun exposure, limiting its use in tropical areas.

questions.question: In which geographic region is doxycycline the prophylaxis of choice over mefloquine?
questions.explanation: In areas with high mefloquine resistance, such as parts of Southeast Asia, doxycycline is preferred for its sustained efficacy against resistant strains of the parasite.

questions.question: What is the main cause of hyperreactive splenomegaly in chronic malaria?
questions.explanation: Hyperreactive splenomegaly in chronic malaria is due to an exaggerated immune response of the host to recurrent Plasmodium infection, with lymphoid and macrophage hyperplasia.

questions.question: What serological finding is characteristic of malarial hyperreactive splenomegaly?
questions.explanation: In malarial hyperreactive splenomegaly, a marked increase in total IgG is observed, due to chronic stimulation of B lymphocytes by parasitic antigens.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for malarial hyperreactive splenomegaly?
questions.explanation: The first-line treatment is prolonged antimalarial therapy with drugs such as chloroquine or sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine, which reduces the parasite load and splenic hyperplasia.

questions.question: What serious complication is associated with malarial hyperreactive splenomegaly?
questions.explanation: Spontaneous splenic rupture is a serious complication of malarial hyperreactive splenomegaly, due to the large size and fragility of the enlarged spleen.

questions.question: What cytopenia is most common in malarial hyperreactive splenomegaly?
questions.explanation: Thrombocytopenia is the most common cytopenia in malarial hyperreactive splenomegaly, secondary to sequestration and destruction of platelets in the enlarged spleen.

questions.question: What epidemiological characteristic is typical of malarial hyperreactive splenomegaly?
options: Affects children under 5 years of age
It is more common in young adults
Predominates in areas of low transmission
questions.explanation: Malaria hyperreactive splenomegaly is more common in young adults from endemic areas with prolonged and repeated exposure to Plasmodium, not in young children or tourists.

questions.question: What main mechanism explains chloroquine resistance in P. falciparum?
questions.explanation: Resistance to chloroquine in Plasmodium falciparum is mainly due to mutations in the pfcrt gene, which encodes the PfCRT transporter, altering the accumulation of the drug in the digestive vacuole of the parasite.

questions.question: What is the treatment of choice for uncomplicated malaria due to chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum?
questions.explanation: Artemisinin combination therapy (ACT) is the standard treatment for uncomplicated malaria due to chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum, due to its high efficacy and ability to reduce resistance.

questions.question: What transporter protein is mutated in chloroquine resistance?
questions.explanation: The PfCRT (Plasmodium falciparum Chloroquine Resistance Transporter) protein is the main one involved in resistance to chloroquine, with mutations that reduce the accumulation of the drug in the digestive vacuole.

questions.question: What additional gene amplifies chloroquine resistance in P. falciparum?
questions.explanation: The pfmdr1 gene encodes the P-glycoprotein homolog 1, whose amplification or mutations can modulate resistance to chloroquine and other antimalarials, although the main mutation remains in pfcrt.

questions.question: In which geographic region is chloroquine resistance in P. falciparum most prevalent?
questions.explanation: Southeast Asia, especially the Greater Mekong region, has historically been the epicenter of resistance to chloroquine and other antimalarials, with a high prevalence of multidrug-resistant strains of P. falciparum.

questions.question: What mechanism prevents the accumulation of chloroquine in the resistant parasite?
questions.explanation: In resistant strains, the mutated PfCRT protein increases the efflux of chloroquine from the digestive vacuole to the cytoplasm, reducing its intracellular concentration and preventing its toxic action on heme.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of primaquine against hypnozoites?
questions.explanation: Primaquine acts by generating oxidative stress and mitochondrial damage in Plasmodium vivax hypnozoites, which allows eliminating latent liver forms and preventing relapses.

questions.question: What parasitic form does primaquine specifically eliminate?
questions.explanation: Primaquine is the only drug available that eliminates hepatic hypnozoites of P. vivax and P. ovale, preventing late relapses of malaria.

questions.question: What enzyme should be evaluated before starting primaquine?
questions.explanation: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency is a relative contraindication to primaquine, as it can cause severe acute hemolysis in these patients.

questions.question: What is the typical duration of primaquine treatment for hypnozoites?
questions.explanation: The standard regimen to eliminate P. vivax hypnozoites with primaquine is 14 days at doses of 0.25-0.5 mg/kg/day, although shorter regimens are used in some contexts.

questions.question: What species of Plasmodium requires treatment with primaquine?
questions.explanation: P. vivax and P. ovale form hepatic hypnozoites that can be reactivated; Primaquine is indicated to eliminate these latent forms, while P. falciparum does not produce hypnozoites.

questions.question: What serious adverse effect can primaquine cause in patients with G6PD deficiency?
questions.explanation: In patients with G6PD deficiency, primaquine can trigger acute hemolysis due to the oxidative stress it generates in erythrocytes, so prior evaluation is required.

questions.question: What is the main etiological agent of malaria transmitted by transfusion?
questions.explanation: Plasmodium falciparum is the most common and dangerous species in transfusion malaria, responsible for the majority of serious cases and deaths associated with this route of transmission.

questions.question: What incubation period is typical in post-transfusion malaria?
questions.explanation: In malaria transmitted by transfusion, the incubation period is usually 7 to 30 days, depending on the Plasmodium species and the parasite load inoculated.

questions.question: What is the most sensitive screening method to detect malaria in blood donors?
questions.explanation: Real-time PCR is the most sensitive technique for detecting Plasmodium DNA in donor blood, surpassing microscopic methods and antigen tests.

questions.question: Which donor population is at greatest risk of transmitting malaria by transfusion?
options: Donors with a history of malaria
questions.explanation: Donors with a history of malaria, especially if they have lived in endemic areas, can harbor latent forms of the parasite and transmit it even years after infection.

questions.question: What is the treatment of choice for uncomplicated falciparum malaria acquired by transfusion?
questions.explanation: The artemether-lumefantrine combination is the first-line treatment for uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria, including cases acquired by transfusion, due to its high efficacy.

questions.question: Which preventive measure is most effective in reducing malaria transmission by transfusion?
questions.explanation: The temporary or permanent deferral of donors with a history of malaria or recent travel to endemic areas is the most effective measure to prevent transfusion transmission of the disease.
